food_name: Rice Flour (plain, raw, from long-grain white rice)

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice flour, white, milled from long-grain white rice
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for plain rice flour as a food ingredient; not a clinical substance requiring SNOMED mapping.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical recipe portion of rice flour is approximately 30g, equivalent to about 3 heaped tablespoons, as used in idiyappam, kueh, or rice noodle preparations.

nutrient_source.title: Rice Flour — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05040011; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Rice flour, white, unenriched, FDC ID 169717)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F05040011 is an exact match for plain raw white rice flour. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for rice flour, white, unenriched (FDC ID 169717). Values are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice flour-based preparations (rice porridge/congee, GI ~78); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dry raw rice flour exists in the University of Sydney GI Database, as GI is measured in consumed/cooked form. GI of 78 is referenced from rice flour-based preparations (rice porridge/congee) in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (78 × 24.03) / 100 ≈ 18.7 for a 30g portion (24.03g carbohydrate).

hypertension.guidance: Rice flour contains no sodium and does not contribute to blood pressure elevation; it is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided that sodium is not added during preparation of rice flour-based dishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~78) and high starch content (80.1g carbohydrate/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice flour has a high GI (~78) and high carbohydrate density; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion sizes of rice flour-based foods, combine them with protein and dietary fat to blunt postprandial glucose response, and consider partial substitution with lower-GI flours such as chickpea or almond flour.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, negligible sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rice flour is low in saturated fat, contains no trans fat or cholesterol, and has negligible sodium, making it a heart-neutral ingredient; however, its high glycaemic index and refined starch content mean that frequent high-volume consumption may contribute to metabolic risk factors for cardiovascular disease in susceptible individuals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rice flour is completely gluten-free and is a safe grain alternative for rheumatoid arthritis patients who are also gluten-sensitive, though it provides only modest anti-inflammatory benefit compared to wholegrain or legume-based flours.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice flour (plain, raw, from long-grain white rice) is a fine white powder produced by dry-milling polished long-grain white rice. It is completely gluten-free, making it a widely used alternative to wheat flour in Southeast Asian and South Indian cuisines. In Singapore and the broader Southeast Asian region, rice flour is the primary ingredient in idiyappam (string hoppers), murukku (fried snack), kueh (Singaporean and Malaysian steamed or baked cakes), rice paper, and bee hoon (rice vermicelli). In South Indian cooking, it is used for puttu, kozhukattai, and various festival sweets. Nutritionally, rice flour closely resembles white rice: it is high in starch (80.1g carbohydrate per 100g), moderate in energy (366 kcal/100g), low in protein (5.9g), and low in fat (1.4g). Dietary fibre is modest at 2.4g/100g, lower than wholegrain rice flour or wheat flour. Sodium is negligible (0 mg/100g). The glycemic index of rice flour-based preparations is high (~78), meaning it can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation. It is distinct from glutinous rice flour (tepung pulut), which is made from waxy rice and produces a chewy, sticky texture — the two are not interchangeable in recipes. Rice flour is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free diets. It contains no gluten and is safe for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity.
common_user_queries: rice flour vs wheat flour nutrition
is rice flour good for diabetics
white rice flour vs glutinous rice flour

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F05040011), an approved primary regional source, with cross-reference confirmation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (rice flour, white, unenriched). Both sources are in close agreement. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for rice flour-based preparations (analogous estimate, confidence 0.9). Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible exact matches.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for plain white rice flour not reported in HPB Foodo F05040011 or USDA SR Legacy at the level of individual unsaturated fatty acid fractions. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this food code; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 11.9g water/100g for unenriched white rice flour but this value was not carried forward as the HPB record is the primary source and does not include moisture. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as rice flour is a plant-derived food with no cholesterol. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy — rice flour is not a source of these vitamins. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on rice flour-based preparations (rice porridge/congee) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for dry raw rice flour exists as GI is only measurable in consumed form. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as (78 × 24.03) / 100 ≈ 18.7. Hindi local name (चावल का आटा (कच्चा)) added per multilingual requirement for Southeast Asian / South Indian cuisine category. This food is a raw ingredient, not a prepared dish; best_meal_times in the original input reflects use-case contexts rather than meal occasions.
